sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 51, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 1, step 51. Player (N, 4x4) at [28,32]-[31,34]. - 3 c frames (same hash) at [24,28]-[27,31], [28,16]-[31,19], [28,24]-[31,27]. Each has a blue box child. - b frame split into 2 parts: [28,28]-[31,31] and [28,36]-[31,39]. - Gray bar split into 2 parts: [29,29]-[30,31] and [29,36]-[30,38]. - Player cuts/splits frames when moving through them. c frames convert to G when player moves toward them, back to c when moving away. - c frames have moved from initial positions - they shift when player interacts with b frame. - Goal unclear - possibly eat all frames, or arrange c frames in specific pattern.
- Plan: First, inspect the segmentation to understand all objects on the board, then determine the goal and appropriate actions.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 50 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the c frame movement pattern:
- Step 3 (UP): c frames went from 3 to 2 (one disappeared)
- Step 5 (LEFT): c frames went from 2 to 3 (one reappeared)
- Step 7 (RIGHT): c frames went from 3 to 2 (one disappeared)
- Step 16 (DOWN): c frames went from 2 to 3 (one reappeared at [28,24])
- Step 26 (RIGHT): c frames went from 3 to 2 (one disappeared)
- Step 28 (DOWN): c frames went from 2 to 3 (one reappeared at [24,44])
- Step 35 (LEFT): c frames went from 3 to 2 (one disappeared)
- Step 44 (RIGHT): c frames went from 2 to 3 (one reappeared at [24,28])
- Step 50 (LEFT): c frames went from 3 to 2 (one disappeared)
- Step 51 (RIGHT): c frames went from 2 to 3 (one reappeared at [28,24])
So the pattern is: c frames appear and disappear based on player movement. When the player moves in certain directions, c frames appear or disappear.
The c frame at [28,16] has always been present. The other c frames appear and disappear.
The c frame at [24,28] appeared at step 44 (RIGHT) and has been present since.
The c frame at [28,24] appeared at step 16 (DOWN), disappeared at step 26 (RIGHT), reappeared at step 28 (DOWN), disappeared at step 35 (LEFT), disappeared at step 44 (RIGHT), disappeared at step 50 (LEFT), and reappeared at step 51 (RIGHT).
This is confusing. Let me think about what the goal might be.
Looking at the current state:
- 3 c frames present
- Player at [28,32]-[31,34]
- b frame and gray bar partially eaten
Maybe the goal is to have all 3 c frames present and the player in a specific position? Or maybe the goal is to eat all the b frame and gray bar?
Let me check if there's a pattern in the number of c frames and the game state. When there are 3 c frames, is the game closer to completion?
Actually, let me look at this differently. The c frames seem to be "targets" that the player needs to collect or interact with. The b frame and gray bar are obstacles.
Looking at the current board:
- c frame at [24,28]-[27,31] (top)
- c frame at [28,16]-[31,19] (left)
- c frame at [28,24]-[31,27] (middle)
- Player at [28,32]-[31,34]
The player is adjacent to the c frame at [28,24]-[31,27]. Maybe I need to move into this c frame to "collect" it?
Let me try moving LEFT to see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- Level 1, step 51. Player (N) at [28,32]-[31,34].
- 3 c frames (same hash) at [24,28]-[27,31], [28,16]-[31,19], [28,24]-[31,27].
- c frames appear/disappear based on player direction. Pattern: LEFT often removes one, RIGHT often adds one.
- b frame split: [28,28]-[31,31] and [28,36]-[31,39]. Gray bar split similarly.
- Player cuts frames when moving through them. Goal likely involves collecting/eliminating c frames or completing the b frame consumption.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check what happens when player moves into a c frame
# First, let me see the exact layout
current = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("Current objects near player:")
for n in current:
    if n['color'] not in ['w','W'] and n['boundary'][0][0] < 60:
        bl = n['boundary'][0]
        br = n['boundary'][-1]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: [{bl[0]},{bl[1]}]-[{br[0]},{br[1]}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Player is at [28,32]-[31,34]
# c frame at [28,24]-[31,27] is to the left (cols 24-27)
# b frame at [28,28]-[31,31] is between player and c frame (cols 28-31)
# So to reach the c frame, player needs to move LEFT through the b frame

# Let me try moving LEFT to see if player can reach the c frame
action(['LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
